gsm_s01.jpg: A professional academic timeline infographic illustrating the evolution of mobile cellular generations from 1G to 4G. The horizontal timeline spans from the early 1980s to the 2010s, with four labeled milestone nodes: '1G Analog (NMT/AMPS)' starting in 1981, '2G GSM (Digital)' starting in 1991, '3G UMTS (Multimedia)' starting in 2001, and '4G LTE (All-IP)' starting in 2009. Each node features a distinct icon: an analog radio tower for 1G, a digital mobile phone with SMS bubble for 2G, a smartphone with video call icon for 3G, and a data-centric tablet for 4G. Below the timeline, technical attribute rows compare each generation across criteria such as 'Modulation', 'Access Method', 'Data Rate', and 'Key Service'. Callout annotations highlight the breakthroughs: 'First worldwide standard' for 2G, 'Packet-switched internet' for 3G, and 'Native IP network' for 4G. The color palette uses professional navy blue, steel gray, and white with subtle accent colors to distinguish the four generations. The background is a clean, neutral white with no decorative elements. All text labels are in English using a clear sans-serif academic font. The diagram is self-contained and serves as a definitive visual introduction to mobile network evolution for a telecommunications engineering lecture.
gsm_s02.jpg: A professional and historically accurate technical illustration of a 1G NMT 450 cellular system from the 1980s. The central focus is a car-mounted mobile telephone installation featuring a handset connected to a bulky transceiver unit in the trunk and a roof-mounted whip antenna. Callout lines with technical labels identify the 'NMT 450 Transceiver Unit', 'FM Modulator/Demodulator', 'Control Handset', and 'Quarter-wave Roof Antenna'. A spectrum allocation diagram in the upper right corner shows the 450 MHz frequency band divided into FDMA channels, each labeled with a 25 kHz bandwidth. A technical annotation reads 'Analog FM Voice - No Encryption - Eavesdroppable via Radio Scanner'. An inset details the limitation of FDMA: one user per channel per call, highlighting the capacity constraints of analog systems. The illustration style is strictly technical line art with realistic shading, using professional grays, blues, and muted earth tones for the hardware. The background is a neutral off-white. No people or decorative elements are present. All annotations are in English, providing a definitive reference for understanding first-generation analog cellular technology.
gsm_s03.jpg: A professional and academic illustration depicting the GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) standard as the defining 2G digital revolution. The image centers on a world map with major economic regions highlighted in blue to indicate GSM adoption across Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia. Four key technology pillars are arranged around the map in labeled blocks: 'Digital Transmission (TDMA/FDMA)' with a diagram showing 8 timeslots per carrier, 'SIM Card Authentication' with an illustration of a SIM chip and cryptographic key flow, 'International Roaming' with an arrow crossing borders between two MSC nodes, and 'Short Message Service (SMS)' with a store-and-forward pathway from phone to SMSC to recipient. Below the map, a comparative callout reads 'From Analog to Digital: Voice Compression, Encryption (A5), and Signaling'. A small reference diagram shows the GSM frequency bands at 900 MHz and 1800 MHz. The color scheme uses professional blues, grays, and white with minimal accent coloring. The background is a clean neutral white with no decorative elements. All text is in English using technical telecommunications terminology. The illustration is strictly academic and serves as a comprehensive visual summary of 2G GSM innovations for an engineering lecture.
gsm_s04.jpg: A detailed technical block diagram of the GSM network architecture showing the three main subsystems and their interconnections. On the left, the 'BSS (Base Station Subsystem)' block contains a BTS tower icon and a BSC controller icon connected by an 'A-bis' interface line. In the center, the 'NSS (Network and Switching Subsystem)' block contains labeled rectangular nodes for 'MSC (Mobile Switching Center)', 'HLR (Home Location Register)', 'VLR (Visitor Location Register)', 'AuC (Authentication Center)', and 'EIR (Equipment Identity Register)', all interconnected by solid lines labeled with GSM-specific interface names such as 'A', 'C', 'D', 'F', and 'G'. On the right, the 'OSS (Operation Support Subsystem)' block shows a workstation and server icons labeled 'Network Management Center' and 'Operations & Maintenance Center'. A legend callout in the lower left explains the subsystem roles: 'BSS = Radio Access (Towers)', 'NSS = Core Switching & Databases', 'OSS = Monitoring & Administration'. The diagram style is strictly academic with clean 2D geometric blocks, professional typography, and interface labels in English. The color palette uses professional tech blues, silver grays, and white. The background is a neutral white. No decorative elements are present.
gsm_s05.jpg: A technical architectural diagram of the GSM Base Station Subsystem (BSS) showing the functional hierarchy between the Mobile Station, BTS, and BSC. The diagram flows left to right: a 'Mobile Station (MS)' smartphone icon connects via a dashed line labeled 'Um Interface (Air Interface - TDMA)' to a 'BTS (Base Transceiver Station)' tower icon. The BTS block includes internal component callouts for 'Transceiver (TRX)', 'Power Amplifier (PA)', 'Combiner', and 'Antenna System'. A solid line labeled 'A-bis Interface (2 Mbps PCM Link)' connects the BTS to a 'BSC (Base Station Controller)' server icon. The BSC block is expanded to show internal functional modules: 'Radio Resource Management (RRM)', 'Handover Decision Logic', 'Power Control', and 'Timeslot Allocation'. A prominent callout highlights that a single BSC manages '50 to 100 BTS units'. An annotation below the diagram explains the division of labor: 'BTS handles RF transmission/reception; BSC handles control intelligence and mobility management'. The color palette uses professional blues, cool grays, and white. The background is a clean neutral white with no decorative elements. The illustration is strictly academic, using clean vector graphics and clear English labels for a telecommunications engineering lecture.
